summary: This iteration tests whether the iter-4 'formula-guided' onset-search strategy, after two targeted robustness fixes, generalizes from 6 development scenarios to a harder 30-scenario benchmark and beats both naive baselines on accuracy (gap_M) and call cost while never violating the throughput-feasibility constraint (gap_f <= 0.02). Four arms compare the upgraded strategy against a constant-max baseline, a blind search baseline, and two ablations that disable each fix individually.
key_points: WHAT/WHY: The benchmark adds three scenario types the dev set never exercised — fully-infeasible queues, occupancy-gap underestimates, and non-monotone interior-peak curves. The hypothesis (h-main) is that anchoring the throughput target at the largest batch size (m_max) plus detecting infeasibility (returning m_min) makes the strategy robust to all three.

EXPERIMENTS: All four strategies run live on the 30 benchmark scenarios. h-main = the two-fix formula_guided; h-control-negative = naive_max (always returns 256); h-ablation = naive_ternary (blind search, no formula); h-robustness = two ablations that each revert exactly one fix (high-anchor and infeasibility detection) to test that each is individually necessary.

CONFIRM: h-main Pareto-dominates both baselines — worst-case gap_M 72 (vs naive_max 255, naive_ternary 145), mean gap_M 14.0 (vs 173.0 and 38.8), calls <=8 (vs ternary's ~30), and zero gap_f violations everywhere. Both fixes must prove load-bearing: reverting the high-anchor should resurrect a gap_f violation on bench-023 (0.004 -> 0.22), and reverting infeasibility detection should re-inflate worst/mean gap_M to ~255/~56.5.

REFUTE: h-main fails if any feasible scenario breaches gap_f > 0.02, if calls exceed 8, or if it does not strictly beat both baselines in aggregate gap_M. A flat (non-load-bearing) result from either ablation would undercut the claim that the specific fixes — rather than incidental tuning — drive the win.

KNOWN LIMITATIONS / HONESTY: A structural gap_M floor exists because the scoring truth is the argmax batch size, not the 0.98-onset the finder targets — e.g. bench-019 onset ~46 vs argmax 118 gives gap_M=72 by convention, not search error. bench-004 (unbounded) is a known per-scenario loss to naive_max (gap_M 38 vs 0). The prior predictor_* cohort is correctly excluded because it cannot even load under the harness; verify this exclusion is acceptable scope.

WATCH bench-025: the one non-monotone scenario where f peaks at M=6 then declines. Both baselines violate gap_f there (~0.0286); h-main is predicted to hold because its m_max anchor sits only 2.86% below peak. If the nc jump depresses f(256) more than predicted on a different grid, the high-anchor itself could breach — the diagnostic plan (anchor over a few probed M instead of m_max alone) is a reasonable fallback.
